narrative:
FIRST WARN FORECAST
Monday, June 29, 2026
Today's Headlines
Dangerous heat continues through at least Friday.
Heat index values will climb between 100° and 110° this afternoon.
Extreme Heat Warning remains in effect for the St. Louis Metro and portions of southwest Illinois.
Heat Advisory continues for the remainder of the First Warn viewing area.
Rain and thunderstorm chances gradually return later this week into the holiday weekend.

The weather story this week is simple: dangerous heat and oppressive humidity continue to dominate the forecast. A strong dome of high pressure remains firmly in control across the central United States, keeping skies mostly sunny while allowing temperatures to climb into the middle and upper 90s each afternoon. At the same time, persistent southerly winds continue pulling warm, moisture-rich air north from the Gulf, creating heat index values that will soar well above 100 degrees, with some locations approaching 110 degrees during the hottest part of the day.

The greatest heat impacts will once again be felt across the St. Louis metropolitan area and southwest Illinois, where an Extreme Heat Warning remains in effect. Surrounding communities continue under a Heat Advisory, but conditions there remain just as dangerous for anyone spending extended periods outdoors. Heat exhaustion and heat stroke can develop quickly, especially for children, older adults, outdoor workers, and those without reliable air conditioning. Be sure to stay hydrated, limit strenuous activities during the afternoon, wear lightweight clothing, and never leave children or pets inside parked vehicles.

The strong upper-level ridge will continue suppressing thunderstorm development through at least Tuesday, meaning we expect several days of dry weather with abundant sunshine. Unfortunately, that also allows the heat to intensify. Overnight temperatures will only fall into the upper 70s, offering little relief after sunset and making it increasingly difficult for homes and buildings to cool down. Tuesday is expected to be one of the hottest days of the week as the ridge reaches its peak strength, with temperatures again reaching the upper 90s and heat index values climbing into the 105 to 110-degree range.

As we move into the second half of the week, the overall weather pattern begins to slowly evolve. While the heat remains firmly in place, the ridge will weaken just enough to allow isolated afternoon and evening thunderstorms to develop. Beginning Wednesday and becoming more likely Thursday through Saturday, scattered showers and thunderstorms will return to the forecast. These storms will be widely scattered and not everyone will receive rain, but those that do could experience brief downpours, gusty winds, and temporary relief from the oppressive heat.

By the upcoming holiday weekend, rain chances continue to increase as disturbances ride along the northern edge of the ridge. Forecast guidance also suggests the ridge will begin shifting westward by Sunday, opening the door for a cold front to approach the region. If that trend holds, a more widespread chance of showers and thunderstorms will develop, followed by noticeably cooler temperatures and somewhat lower humidity early next week. While there is finally some relief showing up on the horizon, the next several days will remain dangerously hot, making heat safety the number one priority across the First Warn viewing area.

Continue checking back with First Warn Weather throughout the week as we track the dangerous heat and monitor the increasing chances for thunderstorms heading into the Independence Day holiday weekend. Stay cool, stay hydrated, and always look before you lock.
